study_area.name: Coyote Valley Biodiversity Corridor
study_area.region: South San Jose and Coyote Valley, California
study_area.story: This demo focuses on the Coyote Creek and Coyote Valley landscape, a real wildlife linkage where wetlands, oak savanna, and urban edge conditions shape biodiversity health.

system_snapshot.title: Source-backed Coyote Valley demo
system_snapshot.summary: EcoScan combines habitat cells with representative field-sensor snapshots anchored to public station locations and species guidance for the Coyote Creek and Coyote Valley corridor.
system_snapshot.observed_window: Representative 2024-2026 public-source snapshot normalized for demo use
what_is_real: Study area, landmarks, and sensor place references are tied to real Coyote Valley and Coyote Creek locations.
Species stress rules are tied to public habitat guidance from California and federal conservation sources.
The sensor values in this sample are representative snapshots mapped into EcoScan's simplified schema for hackathon testing.

location_context.why_here: Coyote Valley is one of the South Bay's most important wildlife linkages and water landscapes, connecting the Santa Cruz Mountains and Diablo Range while buffering flood and groundwater systems.
location_context.pressure_story: The corridor mixes wetlands and creek habitat with dry grassland, oak edges, roads, and urban heat, making it a strong place to demonstrate species-specific habitat stress.

data_sources.name: U.S. Geological Survey - Coyote Creek Outdoor Classroom monitoring site
data_sources.note: Used to anchor the wetland and groundwater monitoring story near Coyote Creek Outdoor Classroom.

data_sources.name: Open Space Authority - About Coyote Valley
data_sources.note: Used for the real location story around wildlife connectivity, water, and flood resilience.

data_sources.name: Open Space Authority - Understanding Coyote Valley
data_sources.note: Supports the presence of wetland, grassland, oak, and wildlife linkage habitat in the study area.

data_sources.name: California Department of Fish and Wildlife - Monarch Butterfly
data_sources.note: Used to define monarch decline drivers, habitat need, and restoration actions.

data_sources.name: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service - California Red-Legged Frog
data_sources.note: Used to define aquatic and upland refuge needs for frogs in creek and wetland habitat.

data_sources.name: Stanford Conservation Program - Western Pond Turtle
data_sources.note: Used to anchor freshwater turtle habitat logic in the regional creek corridor story.

sensor_profiles.label: Coyote Creek Outdoor Classroom
sensor_profiles.summary: Represents groundwater and water-quality context near the Coyote Creek Outdoor Classroom monitoring area.
sensor_profiles.why_it_matters: This station informs frog and turtle habitat health where persistent freshwater refuge matters most.

sensor_profiles.label: Coyote Valley Open Space
sensor_profiles.source_name: Open Space Authority Coyote Valley Preserve
sensor_profiles.summary: Represents mixed oak, grassland, and wildlife corridor conditions in protected open space.
sensor_profiles.why_it_matters: This station helps track edge drying and canopy condition affecting acorn woodpeckers, milkweed, and valley oaks.

sensor_profiles.label: Laguna Seca Wetland Edge
sensor_profiles.summary: Represents seasonal wetland conditions in the southern part of the corridor.
sensor_profiles.why_it_matters: This wetland edge is the clearest refuge signal for amphibians, turtles, and riparian plants.

sensor_profiles.label: Monterey Road Urban Edge
sensor_profiles.summary: Represents the hotter, drier roadside interface where fragmentation and air-quality stress are strongest.
sensor_profiles.why_it_matters: This station drives the urban heat story for monarchs, milkweed, oak recruitment, and shrub cover.
